# Tutorial: MODIS LST vs NDVI with `t.rast.scatter` (Python / Jupyter)

This tutorial downloads MODIS LST and NDVI data and uses them to create an LST-versus-NDVI scatterplot. The study area is the Bardia region of western Nepal, covering part of the Terai lowlands and Himalayan foothills. The workflow uses the **`grass.tools` Python API** together with the **`grass.jupyter`** package for inline map display in a notebook.

The region of interest, covering Bardia and its surroundings in western Nepal, is defined below. To use a different study area, edit the `north`, `south`, `west`, and `east` coordinate values below.


```{python}
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show code"

import folium

# Region bounds

# n, s, w, e = 29.457155, 27.173913, 79.930136, 82.871685
n, s, w, e = 52.1885179883333, 52.1038934705556, 4.73115363583333, 4.87937471166667

# Center the map on the bounding box
m = folium.Map(
    location=[(n + s) / 2, (w + e) / 2],
    zoom_start=8,
    tiles=None,
)

# Base layer 1: OpenStreetMap
folium.TileLayer("OpenStreetMap", name="OpenStreetMap", attr="© OpenStreetMap").add_to(m)

# Base layer 2: Esri World Imagery (satellite)
folium.TileLayer(
    tiles="https://server.arcgisonline.com/ArcGIS/rest/services/"
          "World_Imagery/MapServer/tile/{z}/{y}/{x}",
    attr="Tiles © Esri — Source: Esri, Maxar, Earthstar Geographics, "
         "and the GIS User Community",
    name="Esri World Imagery)",
).add_to(m)

# folium uses [lat, lon]: [[south, west], [north, east]]
rect_bounds = [[s, w], [n, e]]
folium.Rectangle(
    bounds=rect_bounds,
    color="#d62728",
    weight=2,
    fill=True,
    fill_opacity=0.08,
    popup=f"Bardia region of interest\nn={n} s={s}\nw={w} e={e}",
).add_to(m)

# Fit the view to the rectangle
m.fit_bounds(rect_bounds, padding=(20, 20))

# Layer switcher to toggle between OSM and satellite
folium.LayerControl(collapsed=False).add_to(m)

m
```

## Setup

###  Python libraries

The first thing we need to do (for some environments) is to import GRASS Python
packages. In order to do so, we need to add GRASS Python package to path. For a
more detailed explanation of the following steps, see
[this tutorial](https://grass-tutorials.osgeo.org/content/tutorials/get_started/fast_track_grass_and_python.html).

```{python}
import os
import sys
import subprocess
from pathlib import Path
```

Now, make the GRASS Python packages importable (can be skipped in some setups)

```{python}
sys.path.append(
    subprocess.check_output(["grass", "--config", "python_path"], text=True).strip()
)
```

And import the required GRASS Python packages

```{python}
import grass.script as gs
import grass.jupyter as gj
from grass.tools import Tools
```

### Initialize GRASS session

Normally, I start GRASS GIS first and then open my IDE, Positron, from the GRASS command line. The advantage of this approach is that the session is already connected to an existing GRASS project and mapset.

For a tutorial, however, you may not need or want to store the project permanently. In that case, it is convenient to work in a temporary project. That is the approach used here: we create a GRASS project in a temporary directory and use it to import and process the data.

The `gs.create_project()` function makes this straightforward. It creates a new GRASS project and uses an EPSG code to define the coordinate reference system.


```{python}
import tempfile
tempdir = tempfile.TemporaryDirectory()
tempdir = "/home/paulo/Desktop/test/"
```

Create a project in the temporary directory

```{python}
gs.create_project(path=tempdir.name, name="MODIS", epsg="4326")
```

After we create a project, we can start a GRASS session there.

```{python}
session = gj.init(Path(tempdir.name, "MODIS"))

# The Tools object runs every GRASS tool for us
tools = Tools()
```

`gj.init()` starts the session and returns a session object; `Tools()` picks it
up automatically. 

## Addons and credentials

If you have not done so already, install `t.rast.scatter`, which we use later.

```{python}
tools.g_extension(extension="t.rast.scatter")
```

I would have normally used the `i.modis.download` addon for the next step,
downloading the MODIS data. However, the LP DAAC Data Pool that the `i.modis`
addons relied on has been discontinued for MODIS. So, instead we use NASA's
[AppEEARS](https://appeears.earthdatacloud.nasa.gov/) service to download the
data for the study area.

You need a free
[NASA Earthdata Login](https://urs.earthdata.nasa.gov/users/new) and the
`requests` library. We authenticate against the AppEEARS API with those
credentials and receive a short-lived bearer token. When running the code, you
will get prompted for your username and password, so make sure you have them at
hand.

```{python}
import getpass
import requests

API = "https://appeears.earthdatacloud.nasa.gov/api/"

_user = input("NASA Earthdata username: ")
_pw = getpass.getpass("NASA Earthdata password: ")
_login = requests.post(f"{API}login", auth=(_user, _pw))
del _user, _pw
_login.raise_for_status()
TOKEN = _login.json()["token"]
HEAD = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {TOKEN}"}
print("Authenticated with AppEEARS.")
```

Note that you can also download the data manually, as described in [this tutorial](https://ecodiv.earth/post/modis-fire-products-in-grass-gis/).

## Set the computational region

You already defined the north, south, west and east coordinates of your region
of interest above. We now use these to set the region. Use the `-p` flag to
print and check the region settings.

```{python}
tools.g_region(n=n, s=s, w=w, e=e, res=0.0083333, flags="p")
```

`res=0.0083333` (~1 km) matches the native cell size of the 1 km MODIS products
used here. Because `t.rast.scatter` samples at points rather than across the
region grid, the resolution is not critical, but aligning the region with the
data is good practice.

## Download the data with AppEEARS

We request two years (2018–2019) so there are two full annual cycles — enough to
get the full seasonal loop. You can request more.

We use two 1 km MODIS products that AppEEARS delivers as GeoTIFFs:

- **MOD11A2.061** — 8-day daytime Land Surface Temperature (`LST_Day_1km`)
- **MOD13A2.061** — 16-day NDVI (`_1_km_16_days_NDVI`)

### Confirm the layer names

We need the exact layer identifiers, which is the code below for. For each entry in `LAYER_SPECS`, the code below checks whether the `layer` name we expect actually exists for that `product`, and if not, falls back to the first layer whose name or description contains the `match` hint.

```{python}
# What we want from each product: an expected identifier plus a substring hint.
LAYER_SPECS = [
    {"product": "MOD11A2.061", "layer": "LST_Day_1km", "match": "LST_Day"},
    {"product": "MOD13A2.061", "layer": "_1_km_16_days_NDVI", "match": "NDVI"},
]

def resolve_layers(specs):
    resolved = []
    for spec in specs:
        prod = spec["product"]
        layers = requests.get(f"{API}product/{prod}", headers=HEAD).json()
        want = spec.get("layer")
        if want in layers:
            chosen = want
        else:
            hint = spec.get("match", "").lower()
            chosen = next(
                (ln for ln, meta in layers.items()
                 if hint in ln.lower()
                 or hint in str(meta.get("Description", "")).lower()),
                None,
            )
            if chosen is None:
                raise RuntimeError(
                    f"No layer in {prod} matches '{want}'/'{spec.get('match')}'. "
                    f"Available: {sorted(layers)}"
                )
            print(f"note: {prod} '{want}' not found; using '{chosen}'")
        resolved.append({"product": prod, "layer": chosen})
        print(f"resolved {prod} -> {chosen}")
    return resolved

layers = resolve_layers(LAYER_SPECS)
```

### Request the data

An AppEEARS *area* request takes a GeoJSON polygon, which we create using the
bounding box we defined earlier. It furthermore needs the date range (AppEEARS
expects the format `MM-DD-YYYY`), and the layer list. We ask for GeoTIFF output
in geographic projection (EPSG:4326) to match our GRASS project.

```{python}
geojson = {
    "type": "FeatureCollection",
    "features": [{
        "type": "Feature",
        "properties": {},
        "geometry": {
            "type": "Polygon",
            "coordinates": [[[w, s], [e, s], [e, n], [w, n], [w, s]]],
        },
    }],
}

task = {
    "task_type": "area",
    "task_name": "bardia_lst_ndvi",
    "params": {
        "dates": [{"startDate": "01-01-2018", "endDate": "12-31-2019"}],
        "layers": layers,
        "geo": geojson,
        "output": {
            "format": {"type": "geotiff"},
            "projection": "geographic",
        },
    },
}

resp = requests.post(f"{API}task", json=task, headers=HEAD)
resp.raise_for_status()
task_id = resp.json()["task_id"]
print(f"Submitted task: {task_id}")
```

This prints the task order, which will be processed on the AppEEARS servers. Processing can take some time. One option is to wait and check again later, but the more convenient approach is to automatically check the request status repeatedly until it changes to `done`. You may also receive an email with a link where you can monitor the request (I am not sure this is on by default, but I did receive an email with link).

```{python}
import time

while True:
    status = requests.get(f"{API}status/{task_id}", headers=HEAD).json()
    state = status.get("status", "unknown")
    print(f"status: {state}")
    if state == "done":
        break
    if state in ("error", "expired", "deleted"):
        raise RuntimeError(f"AppEEARS task ended with status: {state}")
    time.sleep(60)
```

### Download the GeoTIFFs

The completed task exposes a *bundle* of files. We download the GeoTIFFs (and
skip the README, granule list, JSON, and quality CSVs).

```{python}
modis_dir = Path.home() / "modisdata" / "appeears_bardia"
modis_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

bundle = requests.get(f"{API}bundle/{task_id}", headers=HEAD).json()
downloaded = []
for f in bundle["files"]:
    if f["file_type"] != "tif":
        continue
    fname = Path(f["file_name"]).name
    dest = modis_dir / fname
    with requests.get(
        f"{API}bundle/{task_id}/{f['file_id']}", headers=HEAD, stream=True
    ) as dl:
        dl.raise_for_status()
        with open(dest, "wb") as fh:
            for chunk in dl.iter_content(chunk_size=1 << 20):
                fh.write(chunk)
    downloaded.append(dest)
print(f"{len(downloaded)} GeoTIFFs in {modis_dir}")
```

## Import and register the data

AppEEARS names area GeoTIFFs with a `doyYYYYDDD` date token, e.g.
`MOD11A2.061_LST_Day_1km_doy2018001_aid0001.tif`. We parse that token for the
date, import each file with `r.import`, and register the maps into two strds.

```{python}
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
import tempfile
import re
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

def doy_to_iso(stem):
    """Extract 'doyYYYYDDD…' from a filename stem and return 'YYYY-MM-DD'."""
    m = re.search(r"doy(\d{4})(\d{3})", stem)
    if not m:
        return None
    year, doy = int(m.group(1)), int(m.group(2))
    return (datetime(year, 1, 1) + timedelta(days=doy - 1)).strftime("%Y-%m-%d")

lst_maps, ndvi_maps = [], []
for f in downloaded:
    stem = Path(f).stem
    iso = doy_to_iso(stem)
    if iso is None:
        continue
    if "LST_Day" in stem:
        name = f"lst_{iso.replace('-', '')}"
        tools.r_import(input=str(f), output=name)
        lst_maps.append((name, iso))
    elif "NDVI" in stem:
        name = f"ndvi_{iso.replace('-', '')}"
        tools.r_import(input=str(f), output=name)
        ndvi_maps.append((name, iso))

def register_strds(name, maps, title):
    tools.t_create(
        type="strds", temporaltype="absolute", output=name,
        title=title, description=title, semantictype="mean",
    )
    with tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile("w", suffix=".txt", delete=False) as tf:
        for mapname, iso in sorted(maps, key=lambda x: x[1]):
            tf.write(f"{mapname}|{iso}\n")
        reg = tf.name
    tools.t_register(input=name, file=reg)
    os.unlink(reg)

register_strds("lst_8day", lst_maps,
               "MODIS LST Day 8-day (MOD11A2) - Bardia 2018-2019")
register_strds("ndvi_16day", ndvi_maps,
               "MODIS NDVI 16-day (MOD13A2) - Bardia 2018-2019")
```

### Rescale to physical units

The raw GeoTIFF values are stored as scaled integers. We convert LST to degrees
Celsius (`K * 0.02 - 273.15`) and NDVI to its physical −1..1 range (`* 0.0001`),
and apply sensible colour tables.

```{python}
tools.t_rast_mapcalc(
    input="lst_8day", output="lst_8day_c", basename="lst_c",
    expression="lst_8day * 0.02 - 273.15", nprocs=8
)
tools.t_rast_colors(input="lst_8day_c", color="celsius")

tools.t_rast_mapcalc(
    input="ndvi_16day", output="ndvi_16day_s", basename="ndvi_s",
    expression="ndvi_16day * 0.0001", nprocs=8
)
tools.t_rast_colors(input="ndvi_16day_s", color="ndvi")
```

## Display a map inline with `grass.jupyter`

Before the scatterplot, render one of the maps inline using `grass.jupyter` to
confirm the data look right.

```{python}
# Pick one NDVI map to display (substitute an actual map name from your import)
ndvi_first = tools.t_rast_list(
    input="ndvi_16day_s", columns="name", flags="u"
).text.splitlines()[0]

m = gj.Map()
m.d_rast(map=ndvi_first)
m.d_legend(raster=ndvi_first, bgcolor="white")
m.show()
```

## Create sample points

`t.rast.scatter` samples both datasets at a fixed set of points and at every
timestep, so each plotted observation is a matched (LST, NDVI) pair at one
location and time.

```{python}
# Use one imported LST map as the sampling frame.
lst_first = tools.t_rast_list(
    input="lst_8day_c", columns="name", flags="u"
).text.splitlines()[0]

tools.r_random(
    seed=1,
    input=lst_first,
    npoints=60,
    vector="bardia_pts",
)
```

Whether sixty points is enough to represent the conditions across the region is
something you have to decide (you could use the `r.meb` or `r.mess` addon to
check). For now, the goal is to capture the main variation while keeping
individual months distinguishable by colour.

## Plot LST vs NDVI

Because LST is 8-day and NDVI is 16-day, the datasets do not share timestamps
one-to-one. `t.rast.scatter` pairs them for us. With `method=nearest` each map is
paired with the closest-in-time map of the other dataset within a tolerance,
which keeps every observation as its own dated point and preserves the finest
temporal detail. (The alternative, shown later, bins both datasets to a common
frequency first.)

Throughout, flags are concatenated into one string: `flags="ol"` is `-o -l` (OLS
line plus LOWESS smoother). The rendering flags `-c`, `-y`, and `-D` below are
**mutually exclusive** — each is a different way of showing the same paired
cloud, so you pick one at a time.

### Colour by absolute time (`-c`)

`-c` colours each point by its absolute timestep. This turns the otherwise
ambiguous cloud into a dated trajectory.

```{python}
tools.t_rast_scatter(
    xstrds="ndvi_16day_s",
    ystrds="lst_8day_c",
    points="bardia_pts",
    method="nearest", tolerance="16 days",
    x_label="NDVI",
    y_label="LST Day (C)",
    title="Bardia: LST vs NDVI 2018-2019 (absolute time)",
    flags="col",   # -c absolute-time colour, -o OLS, -l LOWESS
    nprocs=8,
)
```

A drawback for a *seasonal* relationship: the colourbar runs linearly from 2018
to 2019, so the same season in different years gets a different colour (October
2018 is not the same colour as October 2019). A recurring annual loop therefore
shows up as two offset clouds rather than one overlapping ring. The next option
fixes that.

### Colour by season (`-y`, cyclic)

`-y` colours each point by its position **within the year** (day-of-year) on a
cyclic colormap, so the same season in different years shares a colour and a
recurring seasonal loop becomes visible as a closed ring. The colourbar is
labelled by month, and its two ends meet (December wraps to January).

```{python}
tools.t_rast_scatter(
    xstrds="ndvi_16day_s",
    ystrds="lst_8day_c",
    points="bardia_pts",
    method="nearest", tolerance="16 days",
    x_label="NDVI",
    y_label="LST Day (C)",
    title="Bardia: LST vs NDVI 2018-2019 (cyclic season)",
    flags="yol",   # -y cyclic-season colour, -o OLS, -l LOWESS
    nprocs=8,
)
```

Reading the cyclic plot, the cloud sweeps through the year as a coherent
progression: the cool, low-NDVI winter corner (pale tones), a pre-monsoon limb
that climbs to the highest LST at still-modest NDVI (the surface heats before
vegetation greens), and a monsoon/post-monsoon limb at high NDVI with *lower*
LST (evapotranspiration cools the greened surface). For a given NDVI the
pre-monsoon limb sits hotter than the post-monsoon limb — a seasonal hysteresis
the absolute-time colouring cannot show.

### Density (`-D`)

When there are many points, overplotting hides where the cloud is densest. `-D`
bins the points into a grid and colours each cell by the count of points in it.
Set the grid resolution with `bins` (`nx,ny`) and the colormap with
`density_colormap`. A density plot aggregates points of different times into one
cell, so it cannot also carry a per-point time colour — hence `-D` is exclusive
with `-c` and `-y`.

```{python}
tools.t_rast_scatter(
    xstrds="ndvi_16day_s",
    ystrds="lst_8day_c",
    points="bardia_pts",
    method="nearest", tolerance="16 days",
    x_label="NDVI",
    y_label="LST Day (C)",
    title="Bardia: LST vs NDVI 2018-2019 (density)",
    flags="Dol",   # -D density, -o OLS, -l LOWESS
    bins="30,30",
    density_colormap="viridis",
    nprocs=8,
)
```

### Aggregate to a common frequency

Instead of nearest-in-time pairing, you can summarise both datasets to a common
frequency and pair the per-period values. This is cleaner-looking; the trade-off
is that aggregation smooths the loop's limbs slightly. `frequency` takes a pandas
offset alias (`MS` = month-start).

```{python}
tools.t_rast_scatter(
    xstrds="ndvi_16day_s",
    ystrds="lst_8day_c",
    points="bardia_pts",
    method="mean", frequency="MS",
    x_label="NDVI (monthly mean)",
    y_label="LST Day (C, monthly mean)",
    title="Bardia: LST vs NDVI 2018-2019 (monthly aggregate)",
    flags="yol",
    nprocs=8,
)
```

## Add a driver: SMAP soil moisture for the lag examples

The LST–NDVI relationship above is a *relationship*, not a one-way cause: LST
influences vegetation and vegetation (via evapotranspiration) influences LST. For
the **lag** examples we want an unambiguous driver — something that clearly
*precedes* and drives the vegetation response. Rainfall is the textbook choice,
but it is not in AppEEARS. Soil moisture is arguably a better driver still: it is
the variable roots actually respond to (rain matters only once it wets the soil),
and AppEEARS serves it through the same workflow.

We use **SMAP enhanced L3 soil moisture** (`SPL3SMP_E.006`, daily, 9 km, global).
Its daily cadence matters: a lag-accumulation window then has many maps to reduce
over. This section is additive — it reuses the `TOKEN`/`HEAD`/`API`, the region,
the GRASS session, and `resolve_layers`, and creates only a new `sm_daily` strds;
it does not re-download the MODIS data.

```{python}
# Resolve the soil-moisture layer. SPL3SMP_E layers all share the prefix
# 'Soil_Moisture_Retrieval_Data_AM_...', so we name the exact layer we want and
# use a tight match hint so the fallback cannot grab 'albedo' or another band.
SM_SPEC = [
    {
        "product": "SPL3SMP_E.006",
        "layer": "Soil_Moisture_Retrieval_Data_AM_soil_moisture",
        "match": "AM_soil_moisture",
    },
]
sm_layers = resolve_layers(SM_SPEC)
```

```{python}
sm_task = {
    "task_type": "area",
    "task_name": "bardia_soil_moisture",
    "params": {
        "dates": [{"startDate": "01-01-2018", "endDate": "12-31-2019"}],
        "layers": sm_layers,
        "geo": geojson,
        "output": {"format": {"type": "geotiff"}, "projection": "geographic"},
    },
}
resp = requests.post(f"{API}task", json=sm_task, headers=HEAD)
resp.raise_for_status()
sm_task_id = resp.json()["task_id"]
print(f"Submitted SMAP task: {sm_task_id}")
```

A two-year daily request is much larger than the MODIS ones (~730 maps), so the
task and the download take longer. Poll until done.

```{python}
while True:
    status = requests.get(f"{API}status/{sm_task_id}", headers=HEAD).json()
    state = status.get("status", "unknown")
    print(f"status: {state}")
    if state == "done":
        break
    if state in ("error", "expired", "deleted"):
        raise RuntimeError(f"AppEEARS task ended with status: {state}")
    time.sleep(60)
```

```{python}
sm_dir = Path.home() / "modisdata" / "appeears_bardia_sm"
sm_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

bundle = requests.get(f"{API}bundle/{sm_task_id}", headers=HEAD).json()
sm_files = []
for f in bundle["files"]:
    if f["file_type"] != "tif":
        continue
    fname = Path(f["file_name"]).name
    dest = sm_dir / fname
    with requests.get(
        f"{API}bundle/{sm_task_id}/{f['file_id']}", headers=HEAD, stream=True
    ) as dl:
        dl.raise_for_status()
        with open(dest, "wb") as fh:
            for chunk in dl.iter_content(chunk_size=1 << 20):
                fh.write(chunk)
    sm_files.append(dest)
print(f"{len(sm_files)} SMAP GeoTIFFs in {sm_dir}")
```

Import and register as a daily strds. SMAP enhanced soil moisture is already in
volumetric units (m³/m³), so no rescaling is needed. SMAP flags low-quality
retrievals over dense vegetation and open water, so some cells may be null — that
is expected at this 9 km resolution.

```{python}
sm_maps = []
for f in sm_files:
    stem = Path(f).stem
    iso = doy_to_iso(stem)
    if iso is None:
        continue
    name = f"sm_{iso.replace('-', '')}"
    tools.r_import(input=str(f), output=name)
    sm_maps.append((name, iso))

register_strds("sm_daily", sm_maps,
               "SMAP enhanced soil moisture (m3/m3) - Bardia 2018-2019")
tools.t_rast_colors(input="sm_daily", color="blues")
```

## Lag: how vegetation responds to antecedent soil moisture

Vegetation does not respond to soil moisture on the same day; it integrates
moisture over preceding weeks. `t.rast.scatter` can shift the **x (driver)**
dataset in time before pairing, so we put soil moisture on x and NDVI on y. Note
this is a different x/y assignment than the LST–NDVI plots above: here x is the
driver we lag, y is the response.

There are two lag modes.

### Instantaneous shift (`lag`)

`lag` pairs the response at time *t* with the driver at *t − lag* (a single
shifted map). A positive lag means the driver precedes the response. Here we test
NDVI against soil moisture one month earlier.

```{python}
tools.t_rast_scatter(
    xstrds="sm_daily",          # driver on x
    ystrds="ndvi_16day_s",      # response on y
    points="bardia_pts",
    method="nearest", tolerance="8 days",
    lag="1 months",
    x_label="Soil moisture (m3/m3), 1 month earlier",
    y_label="NDVI",
    title="Bardia: NDVI vs antecedent soil moisture (1-month lag)",
    flags="yol",
    nprocs=8,
)
```

### Accumulation window (`lag_window` + `lag_stat`)

A single shifted instant is a crude proxy for "antecedent moisture." The
accumulation mode is more physical: for each NDVI time *t* it reduces all driver
maps in a preceding window. `lag_window` sets the window length, `lag_stat` the
reduction (`mean` suits a state variable like soil moisture; `sum` suits a flux
like rainfall). The window ends at *t − lag*; with `lag` unset it ends at *t*.
Accumulation mode requires the daily driver — which is why we used daily SMAP.

```{python}
tools.t_rast_scatter(
    xstrds="sm_daily",
    ystrds="ndvi_16day_s",
    points="bardia_pts",
    method="nearest", tolerance="8 days",
    lag_window="60 days", lag_stat="mean",
    x_label="Soil moisture (m3/m3), mean of preceding 60 days",
    y_label="NDVI",
    title="Bardia: NDVI vs 60-day antecedent soil moisture",
    flags="yol",
    nprocs=8,
)
```

The optimal lag is not universal — it varies with vegetation type, season, and
climate (see e.g. Ji & Peters 2005; Feng et al. 2023, doi:10.1186/s13717-023-00461-w).
Re-running with a few different `lag` / `lag_window` values and watching which
tightens the relationship is exactly the kind of exploration these options are
for.

## Reading the plots

The LST–NDVI plots show a **loop**, not a line: for a given NDVI, LST differs
between the pre-monsoon warming limb and the post-monsoon cooling limb
(hysteresis). The monsoon months cluster at high NDVI; the dry season sits at low
NDVI with a wide LST spread driven by the lowland–foothill elevation gradient.
Both years overlap in roughly the same loop, confirming a recurring seasonal
cycle — which the cyclic colouring (`-y`) makes far clearer than absolute time.

The lag plots ask a different question: does NDVI track *earlier* soil moisture
better than simultaneous values? Comparing the instantaneous-shift and
accumulation-window versions, and varying the lag, shows how the strength of the
relationship depends on the assumed response time.

A caveat worth keeping in mind: over a region spanning lowland Terai to
Himalayan foothills, a single relationship pooled across that gradient mixes
different regimes. The plots are honest about the scatter this produces rather
than hiding it.

## Notes

- **Calling convention.** Dots become underscores (`t.rast.scatter` →
  `tools.t_rast_scatter`); options are kwargs; flags are a single string
  (`flags="yol"`). A tool that writes to stdout returns a result object whose
  `.text` (or `.keyval` / `.json` for structured output, where supported) holds
  the output; tools without stdout return `None`.
- **Rendering modes are mutually exclusive.** `-c` (absolute-time colour), `-y`
  (cyclic-season colour), and `-D` (density) are three ways of showing the same
  paired cloud; choose one. The regression overlays `-o` (OLS) and `-l` (LOWESS)
  combine with any of them.
- **Driver on x for lag.** `lag`, `lag_window`, and `lag_stat` shift the **x**
  dataset, so put the driver on x and the response on y. Positive lag = driver
  precedes response. Accumulation mode (`lag_window`) needs a temporally dense
  driver and is supported with `method=nearest`.
- **Rescaling matters.** Without the DN-to-physical conversions (Kelvin*0.02 for
  LST, *0.0001 for NDVI) the axes are in digital numbers and the relationship is
  harder to read. SMAP soil moisture needs no rescale (already m³/m³).
- **QA masking.** For a publication figure, request the matching quality layer for
  each product (AppEEARS exposes them, and the
  [Quality API](https://appeears.earthdatacloud.nasa.gov/api/?language=Python#quality)
  decodes the bit flags) and mask low-quality pixels before sampling. Omitted here
  to keep the focus on `t.rast.scatter`.
- **Layer names.** We resolve layer identifiers from the AppEEARS product service
  at runtime rather than hard-coding them, because they differ between products and
  versions. The `SPL3SMP_E` layers in particular share a long common prefix, so we
  name the exact soil-moisture layer and use a tight `match` hint.
- **Task timing.** AppEEARS tasks are processed server-side and can sit in
  `queued`/`processing` for a while; the daily SMAP request (~730 maps) is
  noticeably slower than the MODIS ones. To test the pipeline quickly, shorten the
  date range first.
- **`grass.tools` requires GRASS 8.5+.** On older versions, fall back to
  `gs.run_command("t.rast.scatter", ...)`, which takes the same options as keyword
  arguments and the same `flags` string.
```
